summary: Lynda Resnick discusses her philanthropic approach in California's Central Valley, emphasizing the importance of community-led infrastructure, long-term educational support, and corporate authenticity. She advocates for a 'boots on the ground' approach to giving, arguing that empowering communities and providing education are far more effective than simply writing checks.

principles.statement: Ask the community what they need instead of telling them.
principles.rationale: As an outsider, you cannot accurately guess the specific, everyday fears and needs of a community without asking them directly.
principles.source_quote: we went into every house and conducted an in-home survey to find out what they thought they needed because how would I know what someone in lost Hills would need

principles.statement: Philanthropy requires empowerment and education, not just physical infrastructure.
principles.rationale: Without education and a sense of self-worth, physical improvements to a community will eventually fail and go to ruin.
principles.source_quote: I knew that they had to learn to be empowered that I couldn't stay around and take care of them for life... without education support it was all going to go to dust

principles.statement: Who you work for is more important than what you do.
principles.rationale: Working for someone whose values you don't share is embarrassing and damaging; authenticity is the most important currency.
principles.source_quote: who you work for and with is far more important than what you actually do

principles.statement: Authenticity is the number one currency.
principles.rationale: Society has been hurt by a series of corporate lies, and the new generation (Gen Z) values authenticity above all else.
principles.source_quote: authenticity is the thing that means the most that's the number one currency

frameworks.name: Heels on the Ground Philanthropy
frameworks.when_to_apply: When a company or individual wants to make a meaningful, sustainable impact in a struggling community.
steps: Conduct focus groups and in-home surveys to ask the community what they actually need.
Build the requested physical infrastructure (e.g., parks, lights, storm drains) to address immediate fears and needs.
Implement empowerment classes (ESL, nutrition, exercise) so the community learns to sustain itself.
Invest heavily in long-term education (pre-K through college) to break the cycle of poverty.
frameworks.source_quote: it's easy to write a check but if you really get into the community or the place that you're working you really understand what the needs are and what you should do next rather than throwing money at something

mental_models.name: Heels on the ground
mental_models.description: A variation of 'boots on the ground', meaning being physically present and deeply involved in the community you are trying to help, rather than managing from afar.
mental_models.example: Going into Lost Hills to conduct in-home surveys and directly observing the needs (like basketball courts and storm drains) instead of just writing a check from a corporate office.

heuristics: Don't work for people you wouldn't want to have dinner with.

signature_quotes.text: I realized that I had to do something besides writing checks that I was in a position in life where I could give back in a meaningful way not just support someone else's idea.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the origin of her philanthropic work in the Central Valley.

signature_quotes.text: I knew that they had to learn to be empowered that I couldn't stay around and take care of them for life that you know it is important for people to feel a sense of Worth.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why she added empowerment classes to the physical infrastructure improvements.

signature_quotes.text: Without education support it was all going to go to dust.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why she launched a massive education initiative in the Central Valley.

signature_quotes.text: Who you work for and with is far more important than what you actually do.
signature_quotes.context: Giving career advice to young women about corporate values.

signature_quotes.text: It's easy to write a check but if you really get into the community or the place that you're working you really understand what the needs are and what you should do next rather than throwing money at something.
signature_quotes.context: Describing her 'heels on the ground' approach to philanthropy.

anti_patterns.description: Throwing money at a problem without a firm direction.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It doesn't address the actual needs of the community and lacks the necessary understanding that comes from being physically present and involved.

anti_patterns.description: Working for a company that doesn't share your values.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to a lack of authenticity, potential involvement in unethical practices (like fudging accounting for quarterly earnings), and personal embarrassment.

anti_patterns.description: Telling a community what they need instead of asking them.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Outsiders cannot accurately guess the specific, everyday fears and needs of a community (like needing a basketball court or storm drains) without asking them directly.
